coredata.dc:title: Constructing tungsten doping RuP2@Carbon dots for enhanced hydrogen evolution reaction in acidic and alkaline media
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yingnan
Du, Lingling
Sun, Wenxuan
Zhang, Qi
Su, Yuwen
Sun, Meiling
Yin, Guangchao
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen, a carbon-neutral and renewable energy resource, is alternative to the diminishing conventional fossil fuels energy. However, developing pollution-free electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is desirable but remains a challenge. Herein, a novel nanohybrid consisting of tungsten doped RuP2 nanoparticles (NPs) anchored on the carbon dots (W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0) has been developed for wide-pH HER. Due to the strong coordination interactions between the metal ions and the surface functional groups of CDs, the dispersion improvement of W doping RuP2 NPs is achieved. Meanwhile, CDs form the N-rich self-crosslinking structures that accelerates electron transfer during the reaction. Moreover, the W dopant optimizes the electronic configuration, leading to faster proton shift and hydrogen diffusion. Beneficially, the optimized W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 achieves 10 mA cm at low overpotentials of 19 mV (0.5 M H2SO4) and 28 mV (1.0 M KOH) with an excellent stability. This study provides an innovative idea to design the high-performance non-platinum catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: W doping RuP2
Carbon dots
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Wide-pH electrocatalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic synthesis of the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalyst.

(a) XRD patterns of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 and RuP2@CDs1.0. (b–c) TEM images. (d) HRTEM image of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0. (e–j) HAADF image of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 and the corresponding EDX mapping images of (f) C, (g) N, (h) P, (i) Ru and (j) W elements with the scale bar of 200 nm.
(a) Survey XPS spectrum of as-prepared W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0. (b) the high-resolution spectra of W 4f, (c) Ru 3p and (d) P 2p.

HER performance in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte. (a) LSV curves, (b) Tafel slopes, and (c) Nyquist plots of RuP2@CDsy with various amount of CDs. (d) LSV curves, (e) Tafel slopes, and (f) Nyquist plots of Wx-RuP2@CDs1.0 with various amount of W.

HER performance in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. (a) LSV curves of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0, RuP2@CDs1.0, WP@CDs and commercial Pt/C (20 wt%). (b) Overpotentials of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 at 10 mA cm−2 with other recently reported HER electrocatalysts in 1 M KOH. (c) Tafel slopes. (d) Nyquist plots of electrochemical impedance spectra. (e) Chronoamperometry measurement and the comparison of LSV of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 before and after 3000 cycles.
The HER mechanisms of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1.0 M KOH, respectively.

head.ce:title: Constructing tungsten doping RuP2@Carbon dots for enhanced hydrogen evolution reaction in acidic and alkaline media

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen, a carbon-neutral and renewable energy resource, is alternative to the diminishing conventional fossil fuels energy. However, developing pollution-free electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is desirable but remains a challenge. Herein, a novel nanohybrid consisting of tungsten doped RuP2 nanoparticles (NPs) anchored on the carbon dots (W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0) has been developed for wide-pH HER. Due to the strong coordination interactions between the metal ions and the surface functional groups of CDs, the dispersion improvement of W doping RuP2 NPs is achieved. Meanwhile, CDs form the N-rich self-crosslinking structures that accelerates electron transfer during the reaction. Moreover, the W dopant optimizes the electronic configuration, leading to faster proton shift and hydrogen diffusion. Beneficially, the optimized W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 achieves 10 mA cm at low overpotentials of 19 mV (0.5 M H2SO4) and 28 mV (1.0 M KOH) with an excellent stability. This study provides an innovative idea to design the high-performance non-platinum catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) is a carbon-neutral energy carrier, however, realizing the economic H2-utilization still requires efficient H2 production [1–4]. Currently, the main commercial processes such as the catalytic steam reconstruction, emit carbon dioxide (CO2) that is detrimental to environment [5]. Fortunately, water electrolysis has been proved as a prospective and CO2-free technology for H2 production [6–8], in which the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is one of the two half-reactions of electrochemical water splitting. According to the activity volcano plots, the HER rate is deeply depended on the free energy of hydrogen adsorption [2,3]. To achieve the optimal catalytic activity, the hydrogen binding energy with the catalyst should reach an appropriate value. Although platinum (Pt) is regarded as an ideal catalyst for HER due to the low Gibbs free energy, the scarcity and costly expenses seriously limit its extensive application, and a replacement is urgently needed [9].

Recently, ruthenium (Ru)-based catalysts have aroused wide concerns owing to the outstanding catalytic HER performances that the Ru–H bonds presents the similar strength to the Pt–H bonds (approximately 65 kcal mol−1) besides the lower price [10–14]. What's more, among these Ru-based materials, especially the ones coordinated with C, N, B and P can also display excellent HER capability similar to or even better than Pt catalysts, because of the suitable d-electron configuration and rich chemical state. Especially, the phosphide of Ru is promising in catalyzing HER [15–19], in which the electronegative P atoms can not only act as a base to capture positively protons, but also provide a high activity for the dissociation of H2O to boost HER performance [10,20–24]. Regrettably, the Ru-based phosphides are prone to growing into large bulk counterparts during the thermal treatment, causing a loss to the active sites and catalytic activity.

To hoist dispersion and optimize content of Ru-based nanocatalysts for exposing abundant active sites, choosing appropriate supporting carriers’ ligands to form stable coordination composite with metallic ions is an effective strategy. Notably, the carbon dots (CDs) are potential as such supporting carriers, which is endowed with high electrical conductivity, inexpensive price, and non-toxicity [25–27]. More importantly, multifarious functional groups (such as –COOH, –OH, etc.) on the surfaces of CDs could coordinate with metal ions to obtain a relatively stable CDs-M+ coordination composite, which effectively prevents the agglomerative growing of the nano-catalysts [28].

Based on the above, a novel electrocatalyst with tungsten doping RuP2 anchored on CDs (W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0) has been developed for both alkaline and acidic HER, in which the CDs enhance the electrical conductivity and inhibit the agglomeration of NPs, and the doping of W further regulates the electronic structure of RuP2. With the synergistic contribution, the optimized W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrid presents outstanding HER activity with a considerably low overpotential of 19 mV in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 28 mV in 1.0 M KOH to afford the 10 mA cm−2 current density. Moreover, such W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 electrocatalyst possesses superior durability in alkaline electrolyte over 40 h. Impressively, the HER activity and durability of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 electrocatalyst are comparable to those of the commercial Pt/C, indicating that the low-cost Ru-based phosphides are greatly potential to replace precious metals for catalyzing HER by rational doping engineering.

In a typical synthetic procedure, citric acid (6.3 g) and anhydrous ethylenediamine (2 mL) were mixed with 60 mL deionized water and then transferred to a Teflon liner (100 mL). The mixture was heated at 200 °C in a drying oven for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting brown-red solution was transferred to a rotary evaporation instrument for rotary evaporation. Finally, the product was freeze-dried in a freeze dryer to obtain brown CDs. The resulting CD is usually stored in argon to avoid contact with oxidation or water molecules.

RuCl3·xH2O (200 mg), phytic acid (3 mL) and the as-prepared CDs (y: 0.6, 0.8, 1.0 and 1.2 g) were added to deionized water (20 mL) and stirred to get dark-black solution, which was sonicated for 10 min and quickly transferred to a freeze dryer for freeze drying. Then, the resulting powder was transferred to a tubular furnace and annealed at 900 °C under Ar atmosphere with a heating rate of 5 °C·min−1. After cooling to room temperature, the RuP2@CDsy nanohybrid was obtained. Additionally, the RuP2 particles were prepared without the CDs at the same condition.

RuCl3·H2O (200 mg), phytic acid (3 ml), the as-prepared CDs (1.0 g) and Na2WO4 (x: 0.075, 0.15, 0.3 and 0.6 mmol) were added to deionized water (20 mL) and stirred to get dark-black solution, which was sonicated for 10 min and transferred to a freeze dryer for freeze drying. The other steps were same as the synthesis of RuP2@CDsy, and the Wx-RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrid was obtained. Additionally, the WP@CDs were prepared without the RuCl3·H2O at the same condition.

The W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrid was fabricated via a facile two-step strategy, as schematically depicted in Fig. 1 . The CDs are firstly prepared though a hydrothermal process, in which abundant functional groups formed on the surface of CDs [11,25]. Followed by a physical mixing of CDs, the metal (M: Ru and W) precursors and phytic acid, a relatively secure CDs-M+ coordination was obtained, due to the strong coordination of these M ions and the functional groups (-COOH, –OH, etc.) on the surfaces of CDs. Finally, via the pyrolysis under argon (Ar), the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrid was achieved. In this process, the Ru and W atoms joined to form W doping RuP2 NPs anchored on the CDs. Meanwhile the CDs self-crosslinked to form the graphene-like structure (Fig. S1), owing to the hydrogen bonding between the functional groups and the van Der Waals bonds on the surface of CDs [11,29]. Further, the phase structure of CDs, RuP2@CDs1.0 and W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrids are analyzed by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization. The XRD of CDs in Fig. S2 shows a relatively wide peak at 24°, corresponding to the graphite's (002) plane [29]. After coating the transition-metal phosphides (Fig. 2 a), the obvious strong peaks of RuP2 can be observed at 23.022o, 30.319o, 34.739o, 35.119o (RuP2: PDF#34–0333) [30], which further confirms the formation of RuP2 crystalline phase on the CDs. Notably, no W-related peak is recognized in the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0, while the diffractive peaks of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrids show a slight negative shift compared to RuP2@CDs1.0. This phenomenon implies that partial Ru atoms with small redius are successfully replaced by W atoms with larger radius with the W doping [31].

The morphologies of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrid are observed clearly by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2b–c, the W–RuP2 NPs are homogenously anchored on the surface of the CDs, presenting an average diameter of 7–10 nm (Fig. S3) [10,11,25]. From the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (Fig. 2d)，the clear lattice fringes with the lattice spacing of 0.19 nm are found, which is corresponded to the (002) planes of RuP2 [25]. Additionally, the uniform distribution of Ru, P, C, N and W elements form the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrid is confirmed by the TEM elemental mappings (Fig. 2e–j), which further reveals the doping of W atoms in RuP2 and the uniform coating growth of NPs on the surface of these CDs. It is noted that the N element comes from the ethylenediamine in the formation of CDs Furthermore, the similar morphologies and the uniform elemental distributions (except W) are also observed in RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrid (Fig. S4). By contrast, the RuP2 particles without CDs seriously agglomerate together (Fig. S5), resulting in the number of active sites buried, which would be harmful to the HER performance. The result implies the CDs play an important role in dispersion improvement of the catalysts. Specifically, the various functional groups on the surface of CDs [25], such as the oxygen and amine containing functional moieties, can coordinate with M+ in vacant d orbitals to form a relatively stable CDs-M complex [11,29]. Benefiting from the restricted growth from CDs, these metal ions form dispersed and ultrafine nanocrystals on the surface of CDs in the high-temperature calcination, which effectively avoids severe agglomeration, and thus exposes enriched active sites for HER.

X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) is carried out to deeply explore the surface chemical states and elemental compositions of RuP2@CDs1.0 and W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 nanohybrids. The presence of W, Ru, P, C and N elements is further confirmed by the survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 a). Fig. 3b displays the high-resolution spectrum of W 4f, in which the binding energies (BEs) of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 at 38.4 eV are attributed to the W 4f 7/2 of W6+, whereas the low BEs at 30.7, 31.8, 34.0 and 36.0 eV are attributed to the W 2f 5/2 of W–Ru, metallic W, W–C and W5+, respectively [31]. From the high-resolution spectrum of Ru 3p, one can see that the Ru 3p1/2 (484.9 eV) peak and Ru 3p3/2 (463.0 eV) of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 is negatively shifted by 0.5 eV relative to the ones of RuP2@CDs1.0 (485.4 eV and 463.5 eV) (Fig. 3c) [30], which suggests the W doping effectively regulates the local electron configuration. Similar results were found in XPS of Ru 3d (Fig. S6) [32]. As for the region of P 2p in Fig. 3d, the simulated peaks at 129.5, 130, 133.7 and 134.3 eV are the signals of P–O, P–C, P–Ru and P–W, respectively [33–35]. Obviously, the BEs of P–Ru in W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 is positively shifted by 0.2 eV from 133.5 eV in RuP2@CDs1.0, further suggesting that the W doping prompts electron transfer between Ru and P [31]. In high-resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. S7), three separated peaks are fitted at 284.8 eV for C–C, 285.8 eV for C–N and 289.3 eV for C–O, respectively. These results would provide insights into the relationship between structure and the HER performance.

As is known, the catalysts in the acidic condition are more beneficial to the generation of H2, and thus the HER performance of RuP2@CDs is firstly evaluated in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte to explore the effect of CDs content on the catalytic activity. Clearly, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves in Fig. 4 a show the RuP2@CDs1.0 containing CDs of 1.0 g possesses the superior HER performance than other RuP2@CDs catalysts, exhibiting an overpotential of 78 mV the current density of 10 mA cm−2, though still weaker than commercial Pt/C. The lower overpotential indicates the lower required electron energy during the reaction process. Accordingly, a rather small Tafel slope of 82 mV dec−1 is observed for RuP2@CDs1.0 (Fig. 4b), far smaller than those of RuP2@CDs0.6 (139 mV dec−1), RuP2@CDs0.8 (96 mV dec−1) and RuP2@CDs1.2 (164 mV dec−1), which suggests that the Volmer−Heyrovsky mechanism plays a leading role in the HER. The inferior HER performance of RuP2@CDs0.6 could be resulted from the aggregation of RuP2 particles on the surface of insufficient CDs. Besides, the smallest fitting semicircle diameter of RuP2@CDs1.0 from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots in Fig. 4c prove the admirable electron mobility of which equivalent circuit is the Randall circuit model. These results suggest 1.0 g is the optimized mass for the CDs, and the superior catalytic performance of RuP2@CDs1.0 might been attributed to that the moderate amounts of CDs effectively prevent the agglomeration of the RuP2 NPs, exposing more available active sites, enhancing intrinsic activity and interfacial electron transport.

In addition, to further improve the HER catalytic performance, the various amounts of W atoms were doped into the RuP2@CDs1.0, remarked as the Wx-RuP2@CDs1.0 (x: molar content). Also, the HER performance of these Wx-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalyst was evaluated in 0.5 M H2SO4. Encouragingly, with an appropriate W doping of 0.15 mmol, the HER activity of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 is greatly enhanced compared to the RuP2@CDs1.0 and other W-doped catalysts. As shown in Fig. 4d–e, the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalyst exhibits an outstanding overpotential of 19 mV at 10 mA cm−2 with a smaller Tafel slope (80 mV·dec−1). Further, the exceptional HER behavior of the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalyst can be explained in EIS plots (Fig. 4f), in which a large reduction in the charge transfer resistance (Rct) from ∼40 Ω for RuP2@CDs1.0–∼30 Ω for W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0. These findings reveal the pivotal role of appreciate W doping in enhanced HER catalytic property. On the one hand, the W atoms with the larger atomic radius (2.02 Å) replace the Ru atoms with the smaller atomic radius (1.89 Å), resulting in the original lattice distortion that increases the defect sites of the catalyst for intermediate adsorption. On the other hand, W doping further regulate the electronic structure and accelerates the electrons transfer that is conducive to HER performance [31,36,37].

On the basis above, the HER performance of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalysts was further examined in alkaline media (Fig. S8). Similar trend in catalytic performance was realized (Fig. S8a), when electrolyte switched from 0.5 M H2SO4 to 1.0 M KOH, the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalyst stands out the best among all by affording an overpotential of 28 mV to derive 10 mA cm−2, which was only 2 mV higher than the commercial Pt/C (26 mV@10 mA cm−2) (Fig. 5 a). Meanwhile, the recorded LSV curves in Fig. 5a show that the WP@CDs exhibits the minimal HER activity compared to these RuP2@CDs1.0 and W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalysts, which signifies the dominant contribution of RuP2 nanoclusters to the HER activity, and the W doping further assist to enhance the catalytic performance. Further, Fig. 5b summaries the overpotential at 10 mA·cm−2 for W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 and other recently reported HER electrocatalysts in 1 M KOH (details in Table S1). We can see that our catalyst has the competitive advantage in lower overpotential, indicating the stronger catalytic capacity towards HER. Moreover, the high HER performance of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalyst in 1.0 M KOH was also supported by its comparable. Tafel slope value of 65 mV dec−1 (Fig. 5c and Fig. S8b) and the lower charge transfer resistance of ∼12 Ω (Fig. 5c and Fig. S8c). As reported in previous study, the superior HER performance is also attributed to the covalent bond of P atom and metal atom (M − P), in which the P atoms with strong electronegativity act as the proton acceptor, regulating the d-band center (mean energy) and Femi-energy levels of metal ions, resulting in an improved catalytic activity [31,33,38,39]. Moreover, the W doping can tailor the electronic structure and adsorption property on the surface of the catalysts [31]. Therefore, the synergistic effect between the W doping and phosphides promotes water dissociation and H2 generation.

Besides the high catalytic activity, the electrocatalytic stability is also an imperative parameter for the industrial application of catalysts. The chronoamperometry test for the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalyst was conducted in alkaline media, which shows the current densities of 100 mA cm−2 with little current attenuation over 40 h (Fig. 5e), revealing the excellent endurance at high current density. Meanwhile, the inset in Fig. 5e is that the comparison of LSV curves for W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 measured before and after 3000 cyclic voltammetry cycles remain nearly constant. In order to explore whether the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 structure is stable and the components are reconstructed during the HER process, we have carried out XPS spectrum test on the samples after HER IT test, as shown in Fig. S9. Obviously, except for the disappearance of W–C bond in Fig. S9a, which may be attributed to the dissolution of a little part of C, the other spectrums did not change significantly as compared with that before IT test, which proved that no component reconstruction occurred during the reaction process [21].

These results demonstrate the outstanding electrochemical stability of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 during long-term HER operation. On the one hand, the CDs support effectively protects the RuP2 NPs from aggregation, and promotes the electrical conductivity during the electrocatalytic processes [40–42]. On the other hand, the high-valence W atom as reported is featured with preferable chemical stability in wide-pH conditions as well as high tolerance for poisoning [43]. Besides, the bonds formed between W and Ru enable to restrain the corrosion and dissolution of Ru [37]. Therefore, the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalyst possess the glorious catalytic activity and stability under the synergistic effect.

Fig. 6 is the catalytic mechanism diagram of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 towards HER, which clearly shows the reaction processes under acidic and alkaline conditions. Generally, the HER process usually goes through the following steps in the acidic conditions. The hydrion H+ from acidic electrolyte is adsorbed on Ru site and gains e
- to produce intermediates Hads (Volmer), which further recombines with H+ and e
- to produce H2 (Heyrovsky), or the paired intermediates Hads combine on W site to release H2 directly (Tafel) [44–47]. Similarly, the three steps of HER also occur in alkaline conditions. First, the H2O molecules on the Ru site create intermediates OH− and Hads (Volmer step), and then the intermediate Hads and other H2O molecules adsorbed on W site obtain e
- to create OH− and release H2 (Heyrovsky step). Additionally, the intermediates Hads also inter-combine on W site to release H2 gas in the Tafel step. With the W doping, the electrons might transfer from Ru to W, which facilitates the dissociation of H2O molecules at the electron-deficient Ru site and the release H2 gas at the electron-rich W site, and thus the excellent HER activity is achieved in both 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1 M KOH.

In summary, a novel W doped RuP2 nanohybrid that are loaded on the CDs have been reported for catalyzing HER in both acidic and alkaline condition. Outstanding catalytic activity and durability have been achieved for the W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 catalyst through the synergistic effect from the W doping, RuP2 and CDs supporting carriers. As a result, the optimized W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0 possesses great HER activity of 19 mV@10 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 28 mV@10 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH as well as excellent stability over 40 h. The electronic structure modification from W doping and the interface regulation by CDs supporting carriers greatly improve the catalytic performance of W0.15-RuP2@CDs1.0. Undoubtedly, our study sheds new light on an approach to rationally designing and developing the inexpensive electrodecatalysts with high catalytic performance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully thank to financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61904098, 11904209), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2019QF018).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on fundamentals for designing oxygen evolution electrocatalysts

D.G.J.A.o.c.r. Nocera. The artificial leaf 45 (2012) 767-776.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen production from renewable and sustainable energy resources: promising green energy carrier for clean development

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalysts for the generation of hydrogen, oxygen and synthesis gas

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A general route to prepare low-ruthenium-content bimetallic electrocatalysts for pH-universal hydrogen evolution reaction by using carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, X. Li, Q. Zhang, W. Li, Y. Xie, H. Liu, L. Shang, Z. Liu, Z. Chen, L. Gu, Z. Tang, T. Zhang, S.
